plan.summary: EA9 (MEDIUM, AVAILABILITY, track FFI-PANIC-SAFETY-1): interior-NUL bytes in rendezvous-supplied peer metadata make `CString::new(peer.*).unwrap()` at the FFI boundary abort the whole native app — a zero-interaction remote DoS, since rendezvous is untrusted by design. Fix at the FFI boundary in localbolt-app `native/shared/src/signaling.rs` by mirroring the existing safe `wt_url` handling: map `CString::new` failure to a null pointer instead of `.unwrap()`, for `peer_code`, `device_name`, `device_type` (lines 214-216). The test-only mirror `make_bolt_peer` (lines 343-345) is updated in lockstep — its doc comment declares it a faithful copy of the production path — so the required adversarial NUL-injection tests exercise the fixed behavior instead of panicking in the harness. bolt-core-sdk `signaling_client.rs:62` (the `PeerInfo` struct) is the taint SOURCE cited for context; it is NOT edited — the abort can only occur at CString::new, so the boundary fix fully closes the DoS without perturbing canonical SDK data semantics.

changes.rationale: Root-cause fix. These three fields come from PeerInfo, deserialized straight from untrusted rendezvous JSON. An interior NUL makes CString::new return Err and .unwrap() aborts the process for EVERY peer being enumerated. Replacing .unwrap().into_raw() with .map(|cs| cs.into_raw()).unwrap_or(std::ptr::null_mut()) is the EXACT tail already used for wt_url/wt_cert_hash at lines 205-212, so a malformed field yields a null pointer instead of aborting. The Swift host (BoltBridge.swift:415-417) already null-coalesces each field via `.map { String(cString: $0) } ?? ""`, so a null surfaces as "" — no shell crash, no connectable identity for the malicious peer. Pattern is fmt/clippy-clean (identical to the in-repo wt_url pattern; `unwrap_or(std::ptr::null_mut())` is not flagged by or_fun_call and map_unwrap_or is pedantic-only).

plan.open_questions_resolved:
1) Does the native host tolerate a NULL peer_code/device_name/device_type? RESOLVED = YES. BoltBridge.swift:413-423 reads each field via `peer.<field>.map { String(cString: $0) } ?? ""` (lines 415-417), the same null-safe pattern it already uses for the nullable wt_url/wt_cert_hash (lines 420-421). A null pointer becomes an empty string; the app does not crash and the malformed peer simply has blank metadata (and thus no usable identity to connect with) — the correct fail-safe. So the 'map failure to null pointer' mirror is safe end-to-end; no shell-side change is required.
2) Fix in the SDK (signaling_client.rs:62, as the tracker's remediation note suggests) or at the FFI boundary? RESOLVED = FFI boundary (signaling.rs). The abort can only occur at CString::new().unwrap(); fixing there fully closes the DoS. Sanitizing/rejecting in bolt-app-core's PeerInfo would mutate or drop raw wire data for every SDK consumer (larger blast radius, changes canonical PeerInfo semantics) and is not needed. signaling_client.rs:62 is the taint SOURCE (PeerInfo struct), cited for context only — NOT edited. This matches the task's explicit 'mirror wt_url at the FFI boundary' guidance.
3) Is a non-NUL control char also a trigger? RESOLVED = NO. CString::new fails only on an interior 0x00; other control chars pass through. The adversarial input that matters is a NUL byte, so the tests use `\0`.
4) Will the new tests run in CI? RESOLVED = YES. `mod signaling;` is declared unconditionally at lib.rs:31 and the tests carry no feature gate, so `cargo test` runs them.
5) Are there other CString::new(peer.*).unwrap() sinks to fix? RESOLVED = NO. Grep of localbolt-app/native shows only signaling.rs:214-216 (production) and 343-345 (test mirror); line 275 already uses the safe pattern; daemon.rs/lib.rs use safe patterns or hardcoded test constants.

(c) No vendored `signal/` subtree touched; no SAS/handshake/envelope logic touched or reimplemented; no bolt-core-sdk change. (d) No new dependency. (e) Fix reuses the exact in-repo safe pattern (wt_url), so cargo fmt + clippy stay clean; final whitespace of the multi-line chains is normalized by `cargo fmt` in the verification step (mirrors lines 205-212). (f) EA24 (systemic missing catch_unwind + pervasive lock().unwrap() at the FFI boundary, INFO) is explicitly OUT OF SCOPE — it is a separate finding ('Systemic; see EA9'); this patch does not touch the lock().unwrap() calls. Applier should also add a per-repo docs/CHANGELOG.md line per convention (format not prescribed here since not read).
risks: Behavioral (intended, low): a malicious peer whose metadata contains a NUL now appears in the peer list with the affected field blank ("") instead of crashing the app. Empty peer_code is not connectable, so this is a strict improvement; if the product later wants to hide such peers, filtering blank-code peers in the Swift list is a separate follow-up, not part of EA9.

Formatting: the proposed multi-line method chains must match rustfmt output. They mirror the existing wt_url formatting (statement-indent + 4), but if `cargo fmt --check` flags anything, run `cargo fmt` — it auto-normalizes; verify no unrelated files change.

Test-helper drift: because make_bolt_peer duplicates production logic, a future edit to bolt_signaling_get_peer must be mirrored here too. This duplication predates EA9 and is left as-is (minimal scope); no new duplication is introduced.

Defense-in-depth not added at the SDK: if governance later mandates sanitizing untrusted peer strings inside bolt-app-core, that is a separate, larger, approval-gated change to canonical SDK code and would affect all consumers — intentionally excluded here.

plan.summary: EA25 (MEDIUM, DEP-AUDIT-1) is fixed by four surgical, non-cascading `cargo update -p ... --precise ...` lock bumps in bolt-daemon — NO Cargo.toml or source changes, mirroring the EA10 pattern. cargo-audit (DB with 1160 advisories) confirms the exact solution ranges; read-only `--dry-run` for each package produced a single `Updating` line with zero packages added/removed. Targets: anyhow 1.0.102->1.0.103 (RUSTSEC-2026-0190), rand 0.8.5->0.8.6 AND rand 0.9.2->0.9.3 (both RUSTSEC-2026-0097), rustls-webpki 0.103.9->0.103.13 (clears RUSTSEC-2026-0104/0098/0099/0049; 0.103.13 is the highest required floor). Every advisory has a clean non-cascading fix, so no human decision-to-stop is required. Two findings the task framing understated, resolved by reading the graph: (1) rand is NOT purely transitive — rand 0.8.5 is a DIRECT dep with live call sites (relay.rs:142 rand::thread_rng().gen(); ws_btr.rs/session_loop.rs rand::random()), the exact thread_rng path RUSTSEC-2026-0097 flags, so the 0.8.6 bump hardens real code, not just the audit; (2) anyhow is only reachable on wasm32-wasi* targets (getrandom->wasip2/3->wit-bindgen tooling) which the native daemon never builds, so its bump is audit-hygiene with zero runtime impact. Apply via the four cargo commands (they regenerate checksums); do NOT hand-edit Cargo.lock version/checksum strings.

Both need bumping: 0.8.5->0.8.6 and 0.9.2->0.9.3 (advisory patches: >=0.8.6 for 0.8.x, >=0.9.3 for 0.9.x). 2) Where does anyhow enter the graph? set -euo pipefail

# EA22/EA23 — start.sh must serve a static production build (not the Vite DEV
# server) and must not ship "checksum verification" that is never compared.

SH="start.sh"
fail=0

# --- EA23: production build is built and served; dev server not exposed ---
grep -q './node_modules/.bin/vite build' "$SH" \
  || { echo "FAIL(EA23): 'vite build' step missing in $SH"; fail=1; }
grep -q './node_modules/.bin/vite preview' "$SH" \
  || { echo "FAIL(EA23): 'vite preview' static serve missing in $SH"; fail=1; }
if grep -Eq '\.bin/vite --(host|port)' "$SH"; then
  echo "FAIL(EA23): $SH still launches the Vite DEV server (vite --host/--port)"
  fail=1
fi

# --- EA22: no checksum theater; pinned-TLS fetch retained ---
if grep -Eq 'RUSTUP_HASH|Rustup installer SHA-256' "$SH"; then
  echo "FAIL(EA22): $SH still contains rustup checksum theater"
  fail=1
fi
grep -q "curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs" "$SH" \
  || { echo "FAIL(EA22): pinned-TLS rustup fetch missing in $SH"; fail=1; }

if [ ! -f rust-toolchain.toml ] || ! grep -q 'channel' rust-toolchain.toml; then

fi

plan.summary: EA14: the "unverified -> blocked" transfer-authorization policy is enforced only as UI visibility (localbolt-v3/packages/localbolt-web/src/sections/transfer.ts:43-47 hides fileUploadWrap via isTransferAllowed), with NO enforcement on the receive path and NO code-level enforcement on the send path. An unverified peer (SAS pending) can therefore push file-chunks that WebRTCService.handleMessage routes straight to TransferManager.processChunk*, whose onReceiveFile calls (TransferManager.ts:698 guarded, :742 legacy) auto-deliver/download the file. Fix: enforce the policy at the protocol layer in WebRTCService (localbolt-browser). Add one private allow-list predicate isTransferPermitted() (verified||legacy) and use it to (a) reject inbound file-chunk on BOTH wire paths (enveloped + plaintext) with sendErrorAndDisconnect('INVALID_STATE', ...) before routing, and (b) throw from sendFile() before delegating. transfer-policy.ts is left unchanged (its policy is correct; only its enforcement was incomplete) and sections/transfer.ts is left unchanged (its visibility gate is now a UX affordance backed by real enforcement). NOTE: isTransferPermitted mirrors the state-axis of localbolt-core's isTransferAllowed and does NOT import it, because localbolt-core depends on localbolt-browser (importing back = circular dep + a new dependency needing approval); the task guidance explicitly directs a verificationInfo.state check at this layer. IMPORTANT out-of-scope exposure: WtDataTransport.ts and WsDataTransport.ts have the identical hole and WtDataTransport is a LIVE path in the web app (peer-connection.ts SECURE-DIRECT) — flagged for a sibling finding; not touched here (EA14 scope is WebRTC).

changes.rationale: Single fail-closed allow-list predicate reused by both receive gates and the send gate (keeps the check DRY within the transport layer). Allow-list (not '!== unverified' block-list) so any future/unknown state is refused, matching isTransferAllowed's semantics and the 'mismatch -> BLOCKED (fail-closed)' policy comment. Reads the authoritative this.verificationInfo set by HandshakeManager; does not recompute SAS.

changes.locator: WebRTCService.ts:713-719 (enveloped file-chunk path, insert after the !inner.filename check, before the BTR-4 extraction)
changes.rationale: Primary receive-side gate. The enveloped path is where 'unverified' actually occurs (SAS requires identity keys + envelope), so this closes the real auto-download vector. Placed after the existing filename validation and immediately before this.transfer.routeInnerMessage(inner) (line 724), so onReceiveFile is never reached. risks: BEHAVIOR/UX change (inherent to fail-closed SAS enforcement): a receiver whose SAS is still pending ('unverified') now rejects AND disconnects on an inbound file-chunk instead of silently auto-downloading. In the honest first-contact flow, if peer A verifies and sends before peer B has verified, B tears the connection down and both must reconnect. The added send-side gate reduces this (a peer will not send while ITS OWN state is unverified) but cannot fully prevent it, because a verified sender does not know the receiver's verification state. A softer 'drop-without-disconnect' is NOT actually softer: any error frame we send makes the peer's error handler disconnect anyway (WebRTCService.ts:694-695 / 748-750), so 'send ERROR' implies teardown. Flag to PM: acceptable per the stated policy, but is a visible change from today's (vulnerable) auto-download.

plan.summary: EA4 (HIGH): localbolt-app native bridge spawns bolt-daemon with `--pairing-policy allow` bound to 0.0.0.0, so the WS/QUIC answerer auto-pairs ANY inbound LAN peer (enforce_session_trust returns Some(AllowOnce) under Allow, session_loop.rs:110) and receives files into ~/Downloads with zero consent. OPEN QUESTION RESOLVED: the Swift UI DOES drive a pairing decision over IPC — BoltBridge.swift defines PairingRequest (:441), ingests the daemon `daemon://pairing-request` event (:833-841), and sendPairingDecision() (:754-780) emits `pairing.decision` (allow_once/deny_once) via bolt_ipc_send_decision; LocalBoltApp.swift presents PairingRequestView with Accept/Decline (:1233-1285, shown at :225). Per guidance, the minimal fix is to spawn with `ask` (daemon.rs:91). Fail-closed CONFIRMED: under Ask the answerer denies any identity not already pinned (session_loop.rs:115-123), already unit-tested at session_loop.rs:1780. CRITICAL NUANCE (flagged as top risk): the interactive prompt emitter check_pairing_approval (trust.rs:330) has NO production callers, and no production path ever persists an AllowAlways pin, so `ask` denies ALL currently-unpinned inbound sessions WITHOUT showing the Swift pairing sheet — secure, but regresses browser->desktop and native->native receive until a follow-up wires the prompt into the answerer. Do NOT touch the 0.0.0.0 bind (required for LAN; the policy now gates acceptance). EA8 (MEDIUM): daemon.rs:73 uses a predictable, world-writable /tmp/bolt-native-<pid> data_dir for the durable identity.key + pins/trust.json, then remove_dir_all's it on stop (:471) — so the X25519 identity is regenerated every launch (breaks TOFU pins) and lives in a guessable /tmp path. Fix: point --data-dir at bolt_app_core::platform::default_data_dir() (per-user 0700; ALREADY a dependency, already used at lib.rs:49 — no new dep) and stop wiping the durable dir on stop, cleaning only the ephemeral per-pid socket. Keep create_dir_all + chmod 0700 (:77-83); the socket stays per-pid in /tmp; signal files self-clean at runtime (daemon remove_file after consume: main.rs:618/642/679/760/779/783).

risks: EA4 TOP RISK / behavior change: `ask` fails closed but the interactive Swift pairing sheet is NOT reached for inbound sessions. The WS/QUIC answerer calls enforce_session_trust (session_loop.rs:1150/818), which under Ask only allows an already-pinned identity and otherwise hard-denies; the interactive emitter check_pairing_approval (trust.rs:330) has NO production callers, and NO production code persists an AllowAlways pin. Net effect: after this change, every currently-unpinned inbound peer (browser->desktop AND native->native receive) is DENIED silently. This is the correct secure/fail-closed direction for a HIGH finding, but it is a real inbound-receive regression. FOLLOW-UP (separate finding, out of EA4's minimal scope): wire the pairing.request prompt into the WS/QUIC answerer (call check_pairing_approval / persist an AllowAlways pin on the app's Accept) so users can approve inbound peers via the existing sheet. Schedule this alongside EA4 if prompted inbound receive must keep working.

EA4: do NOT change the 0.0.0.0 bind. Loopback-only would break the app's entire LAN transfer purpose; the pairing policy (now `ask`) is the correct acceptance gate. The interface-binding fallback in the guidance applies only to the 'UI does not handle decisions' branch, which is not the case here.

EA8: bolt_app_core::platform::default_data_dir() is a FIXED per-user path (not per-pid), so two concurrently running app instances would share the same signal-file names in that dir and could race each other's control signals. IPC stays isolated (socket remains per-pid). This is unlikely for a single-instance desktop app with the daemon parent-death watchdog, but is a behavior change from per-pid isolation. Fully separating durable state from ephemeral signals would require a new daemon `--signal-dir`/scratch flag in bolt-daemon (cross-repo, out of minimal scope) — track as optional follow-up.

EA8 narrow replay edge: a send_file.signal written but not consumed before an abnormal daemon exit would now persist in the durable dir and could be re-consumed on next launch. This is gated by EA4 (no unpinned peer can establish a session to send to), so impact is minimal; note it if EA8 lands before EA4.

EA8 optional hardening NOT included (to stay minimal): identity_store.rs could additionally reject a symlinked parent, verify uid==geteuid, and create the key with O_EXCL|0600. Moving off /tmp to a per-user 0700 dir already mitigates most of the TOCTOU/symlink surface, so this is defense-in-depth follow-up, not required for EA8.

plan.summary: EA17 (MEDIUM, AVAILABILITY / DAEMON-PARSE-PANIC-1): parse_transfer_id_bytes in bolt-daemon/src/ws_validation.rs guards on tid.len() != 32, but str::len() counts BYTES, not chars. A 32-BYTE non-ASCII transferId passes the guard, then the loop slices &tid[i*2..i*2+2] (line 116) inside a multi-byte UTF-8 char and panics ("byte index N is not a char boundary"). transfer_id is attacker-controlled from the BTR envelope on a WS frame and reaches this function via ws_btr::decrypt_chunk_btr (lines 107, 131) and session_loop.rs:266, so the panic is network-reachable. Fix: implement the guidance's option (c) — slice with str::get(..) instead of the indexing operator, which returns None (not a panic) on a non-char-boundary/out-of-bounds range, and map that to a validation Err. All three callers already propagate Err via ?/map_err (fail-closed), so no caller changes are needed. Valid 32-char ASCII hex input is byte-for-byte unchanged (get returns Some(same slice)). Change is confined to this one function plus two co-located tests. No new dependencies.

The length guard at line 111 already forbids out-of-bounds, so None now means only a non-ASCII/non-char-boundary transferId, which is correctly rejected. Loop structure and the existing hex-parse error path are unchanged; `i*2` arithmetic is preserved so clippy::needless_range_loop stays silent. For a valid 32-char ASCII hex string every char is 1 byte, so get(i*2..i*2+2) yields Some(&tid[i*2..i*2+2]) identical to the old slice — behavior for valid input is bit-identical (existing parse_transfer_id_valid test still passes).

plan.summary: EA7 (DAEMON-RX-BOUND-1, AVAILABILITY, MEDIUM): the receive/reassembly loop in run_read_loop only checks the attacker-DECLARED file_size against MAX_TRANSFER_SIZE on the first chunk, then buffers unbounded actual bytes (session_loop.rs:1532 guard, :1575 insert), so a peer that declares a tiny file_size but streams huge chunk data OOMs the daemon. Fix: track a running sum of actually-buffered plaintext bytes per transfer and abort when it would exceed MAX_TRANSFER_SIZE; reject chunk_index >= total_chunks; bound total_chunks (MAX_TOTAL_CHUNKS) and concurrent transfers (MAX_ACTIVE_RECEIVES); and size the assembly Vec from real buffered bytes, never the declared size. The per-chunk decision is extracted into a pure, unit-testable helper admit_chunk() in the ws_validation validation-boundary module; the loop change is confined to the FileChunk handler and does not touch HELLO/trust/legacy paths. No new dependencies.

Returning the new running total lets the caller store it. This is the unit-testable surface required by the MEDIUM evidence tier.

risks: Duplicate chunk_index (same index sent twice) inflates the running buffered_bytes sum via naive addition even though chunks.insert overwrites, so a duplicate-heavy stream could abort early. RESOLVED as non-impacting: the daemon sender (session_loop.rs:240-262) emits each index exactly once and pause/resume advances rather than re-sends, so legitimate transfers never duplicate; only misbehaving/attacking peers hit the conservative early abort (which is the safe outcome).

MAX_TOTAL_CHUNKS=200_000 assumes the 16 KiB profile chunk size (confirmed: bolt-core::constants::DEFAULT_CHUNK_SIZE == bolt-transfer-core DEFAULT_CHUNK_SIZE == daemon sender == 16_384). A full 2.5 GB transfer is ~152,588 chunks, so there is headroom; but a future sender negotiating a smaller chunk size for a near-2.5 GB file could exceed 200,000 chunks and be rejected — bump the const if a sub-16 KiB chunk size is ever introduced.

MAX_ACTIVE_RECEIVES=8 caps concurrent inbound transfers per session; a client legitimately fanning out >8 simultaneous inbound files would have the 9th rejected until one completes. The daemon holds one session at a time (module invariant), so 8 is generous, but bump if a UI needs more parallel inbound transfers.

On abort the plan removes BOTH the active_receives entry and the btr_receive_contexts entry, unlike the existing path-escape abort at :1634 which drops only active_receives. It intentionally does NOT call BtrEngine::end_transfer() (matching the lighter path-escape abort) to avoid deeper BTR coupling in the receive loop; the receive-side per-transfer context is the state that matters and it is dropped.

plan.open_questions_resolved: 1) 'Does any UI/other handler depend on Vec::with_capacity(file_size) semantics?' — No. The assembly buffer (session_loop.rs:1613) is local to the FileChunk handler; capacity only affects pre-allocation, not correctness (extend_from_slice grows as needed). The file_size field is retained and still drives progress reporting (:1584-1585,:1604) and transfer.started (:1565). 2) 'Do legitimate senders emit zero-length chunks or duplicate indices (which would need special handling)?' — No. The sender computes total_chunks via div_ceil(file_size, 16384): a 0-byte file yields total_chunks=0 (no chunks sent) and non-zero files never emit an empty trailing chunk; each index is sent exactly once. So no zero-length special case is needed and the conservative running-sum accounting is safe. 3) 'Chunk size for sizing MAX_TOTAL_CHUNKS' — confirmed DEFAULT_CHUNK_SIZE=16_384 across bolt-core, bolt-transfer-core, bolt-protocol-wasm, and the daemon sender, so 200_000 has ~31% headroom over the 152,588-chunk maximum. 4) 'Symbol collisions' — admit_chunk / MAX_TOTAL_CHUNKS / MAX_ACTIVE_RECEIVES do not exist yet; the unrelated quic_transport.rs buffered_bytes() methods are on a different type and do not collide with the new local struct field.
plan.evidence_tier_met: MEDIUM — UNIT + ADVERSARIAL. Adds admit_chunk() unit tests (accept, index-range reject, total_chunks-range reject) plus adversarial tests (cross-cap reject, u64 overflow saturation, and the exact EA7 scenario of a tiny declared size with cumulative streamed bytes exceeding MAX_TRANSFER_SIZE). Tests are un-feature-gated and run under CI's default [transport-ws] features.

concerns: [BLOCKER] MAX_ACTIVE_RECEIVES does NOT bound transfer_id proliferation for BTR transfers, and the plan's NEW cap-reject path introduces a resource leak. In the FileChunk handler, decrypt_chunk_btr runs UNCONDITIONALLY at session_loop.rs:1503-1514 (before the plan's new is_new/cap checks) and, on any chain_index=0 chunk, calls BtrEngine::begin_transfer_receive_with_key and inserts a per-transfer BtrTransferContext into btr_receive_contexts (ws_btr.rs:126). That map is removed ONLY on completion (session_loop.rs:1677) and on the plan's new admit_chunk-Err path. But the plan's TWO is_new reject `continue` paths — the declared-size reject (file_size>MAX, pre-existing) and the NEW MAX_ACTIVE_RECEIVES cap reject — both `continue` WITHOUT removing btr_receive_contexts[transfer_id]. So a malicious authenticated BTR peer sends chain_index=0 chunks for unbounded distinct transfer_ids (each declaring file_size>MAX, or each arriving after 8 incomplete transfers fill the cap); every one leaks a btr_receive_contexts entry (ratchet key material, hundreds of bytes) that MAX_ACTIVE_RECEIVES never bounds. The plan's claim (const doc + governance_notes + open_questions) that MAX_ACTIVE_RECEIVES 'bounds transfer_id proliferation within a session' is FALSE for the primary (BTR) transfer mode. The cap the plan adds only bounds active_receives (the static-NaCl map); the dominant per-transfer-id memory for BTR bypasses it. Worse, the cap-reject path is a NEW create-then-abandon leak site the plan itself introduces, inconsistent with the plan's own admit_chunk-Err cleanup that DOES remove the entry.

[EVIDENCE GAP] The tests exercise ONLY the pure admit_chunk helper, never the session_loop wiring. plan.summary: Minimal, contained hardening of the canonical signaling server (bolt-rendezvous only; no vendored-subtree, protocol, SAS, or handshake changes; zero new dependencies). EA6 (HIGH, single-host DoS): (1) bound the entire pre-registration phase (peek + WS handshake + first register) with ONE shared 10s deadline via tokio::time::timeout_at, closing + releasing slots on expiry; (2) add a per-IP concurrent-connection cap (64) in RoomManager, keyed on the resolved IP BEFORE the private->"local" collapse so each distinct LAN device keeps its own budget while no single host can drain the 256 global pool. EA12 (MEDIUM, slow-reader memory exhaustion): (1) change the per-peer outbound channel from unbounded to a bounded mpsc::channel(64) and switch every enqueue to try_send (sync, drop-on-full — never .await under the DashMap lock; never disconnect a flooded victim); (2) add a 16 KiB signal/manual_signal payload cap far below the 1 MiB frame cap. Together these turn per-peer queue memory from unbounded into a finite ~64 x 16 KiB = 1 MiB bound. handle_connection's signature is unchanged (per-IP state rides on the already-passed RoomManager), so lib.rs:175 and all clients/wire-protocol are untouched. EA11 (subtree sync into localbolt/localbolt-app) is a git subtree pull AFTER this lands — explicitly NOT part of this plan.
plan.evidence_tier_met: EA6 HIGH = INTEROP + ADVERSARIAL: INTEROP `interop_register_and_relay` (real SignalingServer + real tokio-tungstenite client register+relay through the new deadline/guard/bounded-channel paths); ADVERSARIAL `per_ip_slot_cap_enforced` (many connections from one IP -> cap rejects) and `registration_timeout_fires_on_no_activity` (connect-and-never-register -> deadline fires); optional stronger `per_ip_cap_rejects_flood` (65 real conns) and `stalled_pre_registration_is_closed` (#[ignore], ~10s). EA12 MEDIUM = UNIT + ADVERSARIAL: UNIT `signal_size_within/exceeds_limit` + `signal_cap_is_far_below_frame_cap`; ADVERSARIAL `relay_flood_to_slow_reader_is_bounded` (undrained receiver flooded -> queue bounded, excess dropped). All required tests are deterministic and CI-runnable via `cargo test` (bolt-rendezvous default features); no transport-quic gating involved.

risks: Per-IP cap keys on the resolved IP; users behind carrier-grade NAT / a shared public IP already collapse into one room today (pre-existing) and would now also share a 64-connection budget. Mitigation: 64 is generous for a household/NAT; raise MAX_CONNECTIONS_PER_IP for deployments expecting many legit clients behind one public IP. Same failure class as existing room grouping.

A misconfigured public deployment where the proxy forwards no Fly-Client-IP/XFF makes all clients appear as the proxy IP -> they share one per-IP budget. Identical failure mode to existing room assignment; mitigate via TRUSTED_PROXIES / running on Fly (Fly-Client-IP is unspoofable).

Bounded relay queue drops on overflow: a peer already 64 messages behind loses further relays AND peer_joined/peer_left notices until it drains (no resync). Only affects already-badly-behind or malicious clients; legit signaling bursts are far below 64. Verified acceptable.

SIGNAL_MSG_MAX_BYTES=16 KiB rejects unusually large SDP (many codecs / audio+video). localbolt is data-channel-only (SDP ~1-2 KiB), so safe; documented and tunable if the server is reused for richer signaling.

Per-peer queue memory is now finite (~64 x 16 KiB ~= 1 MiB/peer; ~256 MiB across the 256-connection default pool only under active attack on many peers) versus unbounded before. Lower RELAY_CHANNEL_CAPACITY and/or SIGNAL_MSG_MAX_BYTES for a tighter bound on a small VPS.

The shared pre-registration deadline also wraps the WebSocket handshake (server.rs:255), beyond the two lines literally cited by EA6 (:191, :331). This is required — an unbounded handshake is a trivial bypass of the timeout — but reviewers who want a strictly literal minimal diff can drop that one wrap (leaving the DoS partially open). Flagged for sign-off.

Interop tests bind an ephemeral port via drop-then-rebind (tiny TOCTOU) and dial with a retry loop; standard test scaffolding, low flake risk. The optional 65-connection and ~10s stall tests are heavier — keep the #[ignore] on the stall test to keep CI fast.
plan.open_questions_resolved: Q: Is a product/UI handler affected (does a downstream client need changes)? A: No. PeerSender and the per-peer channel are internal to bolt-rendezvous; the wire protocol (bolt-rendezvous-protocol ClientMessage/ServerMessage) is unchanged, and handle_connection's signature is unchanged (per-IP state rides on the already-passed RoomManager, so lib.rs:175 is untouched). Clients (localbolt, localbolt-app, SDK) need no change. — Q: Can the interop test use a real WS client without a new dependency? A: Yes. tokio-tungstenite 0.24 (default features include `connect`, confirmed) and futures-util are already [dependencies] of bolt-rendezvous and are usable from an in-crate #[cfg(test)] module (which links the lib's [dependencies]); placing the interop test in src/server.rs's test module avoids any Cargo.toml change. — Q: Does dashmap 6.1 support remove_if for the per-IP cleanup? A: Yes, signature confirmed: remove_if(&self, key, |&K,&V|->bool)->Option<(K,V)>. — Q: Where can the per-IP cap be enforced? A: Only inside handle_connection, AFTER the handshake+header extraction resolves the effective IP (lib.rs's pre-spawn slot acquire only has the socket addr, which is the proxy on PaaS); keyed on raw_ip (pre-'local'-collapse). — Q: Does bounding the channel force async .send (backpressure while holding the DashMap lock)? A: Avoided entirely by using try_send (sync, drop-on-full) at every enqueue; no .await is added and no lock is held across an await. — Q: EA11? A: Out of scope here; after EA6/EA12 land in this canonical server, EA11 is a `git subtree pull --prefix=signal` into localbolt and localbolt-app. Do NOT edit those vendored copies.

plan.summary: EA5 (High): the TOFU pin store (identity/pin-store.ts + its runtime duplicate webrtc/pin-verify.ts) is keyed on the ephemeral/rendezvous-controlled peer code, so key continuity never forms (WebRTC rotates the code; WS/WT hardcode it to 'ws-daemon'/'wt-daemon', collapsing all daemons onto one pin). Fix: re-key the store on the remote identity public key (base64 of raw 32 bytes) per PROTOCOL.md §2 line 102 and in parity with bolt-daemon trust.rs (keyed by identity_key_hex). risks: Design decision (documented, not a coin-flip): peer code is RETAINED as a non-authoritative label, not dropped. PROTOCOL.md §2 simultaneously mandates identity keying (line 102) AND a reachable KEY_MISMATCH abort (lines 92-98). Pure-identity keying (closest to bolt-daemon trust.rs) makes the mandatory KEY_MISMATCH unreachable; pure peer-code keying breaks continuity. The identity-authoritative + peer-code-label design is the only one satisfying both and the two required adversarial tests. If the reviewer prefers exact daemon parity (pure identity keying, unknown key -> first-contact SAS instead of hard abort), adversarial test 1 must be relaxed to assert 'first contact / unverified' rather than KEY_MISMATCH.

WS/WT use a CONSTANT peer-code label ('ws-daemon'/'wt-daemon'). A user who connects over WS/WT to MULTIPLE distinct daemons will get a fail-closed KEY_MISMATCH on the second (different-identity) daemon. This is fail-CLOSED (safe) and, for the common single-local-daemon case, is the desired 'your daemon re-keyed' warning. If multi-daemon-over-WS is a real workflow, a follow-up could derive the label from daemonUrl; not needed for this fix.

v1 identity keys are X25519, exchanged in HELLO but NOT signature-authenticated (PROTOCOL.md §2: 'authenticated by prior trust, not signatures; v2: Ed25519 mutual auth'). Under identity keying, anyone who knows a peer's PUBLIC identity key and completes the ephemeral handshake gets 'verified' status without re-SAS. This is a documented v1 limitation, orthogonal to this fix; the prior peer-code keying's incidental friction was worthless against the untrusted-rendezvous threat model (rendezvous controls peer codes). SAS remains the active-MITM check; v2 signatures close the gap.

One-time re-pair on upgrade: existing IndexedDB entries are keyed by peer code and lack a value.peerCode field, so they are ignored by the new identity lookup (parity with trust.rs 'legacy entries remain but ignored'). Users re-verify each peer once via the existing SAS/re-pair UI. No stale-entry false KEY_MISMATCH (old value shape has no peerCode to match the getAll filter).

verifyPinnedIdentity is duplicated in identity/pin-store.ts and webrtc/pin-verify.ts (the latter exists to break a circular import). BOTH must be edited identically or the WebRTC/WS/WT runtime path diverges from the unit-tested store. The plan edits both; a future Phase-3 consolidation should delete the duplicate.

Test-surface breadth: the setPin/getPin/markVerified signature change forces mechanical migration of verification.test.ts and the h5-tofu mock. These are required (they assert the vulnerable peer-code keying), not drive-by refactors, but they enlarge the diff. Run the full localbolt-browser + localbolt-web suites to catch any other direct pin-API callers.

MINIMAL/scoped: only the pin-store keying (EA5) and the WS/WT pin-store threading (EA13) are touched; no unrelated systems. No vendored signal/ edits (all changes are in localbolt-v3 packages). SAS/handshake/envelope logic is NOT reimplemented — the shared KeyMismatchError from @the9ines/bolt-core is reused, and pin STORAGE is explicitly a 'transport concern' per bolt-core rust identity.rs:13, so implementing it in the product repo is correct and in parity with bolt-daemon trust.rs (keyed by identity_key_hex). plan.open_questions_resolved: 1) Canonical pin key: PROTOCOL.md §2 line 102 = remote identity public key (raw 32 bytes); friendly name is a non-authoritative mapping (line 103). 2) Rust parity: bolt-daemon/src/ipc/trust.rs:95-99 keys the trust store by identity_key_hex (hex of raw 32-byte identity key) and ignores legacy peer-id entries with no migration; bolt-core/src/identity.rs:13 states 'No TOFU pin storage (transport concern)', so the product repo owns pin keying and only KeyMismatchError is shared. This confirms identity keying and the no-migration approach. 3) UI verify handler EXISTS: peer-connection.ts:681-689 createVerificationStatus.onMarkVerified dispatches markPeerVerified() to directTransportRef (WS) / wtTransportRef (WT) / rtcServiceRef (WebRTC) — so persisting verification inside each transport's markPeerVerified is the correct, sufficient wiring; no new UI handler needed. 4) KEY_MISMATCH is a canonical wire error (bolt-core-browser/src/errors.ts:55, in WIRE_ERROR_CODES), so WsDataTransport/WtDataTransport.sendErrorAndDisconnect('KEY_MISMATCH', ...) emits the error frame before disconnecting rather than silently dropping. 5) verifyPinnedIdentity is duplicated (identity/pin-store.ts + webrtc/pin-verify.ts, used by HandshakeManager to avoid a circular import) — both must be updated, resolved in the plan. 6) WS/WT currently omit pinStore from wsOptions (WsDataTransport.ts:127-131, WtDataTransport.ts:240-244), which is exactly why processHello's TOFU block is dead there (EA13 root cause), confirmed by reading the constructors and context bridges (getRemoteIdentityKey is exposed on both).
